column7: message

I'd say it's not at all about awareness. It's about presence. Plenty of people know there's a lot of people who don't like Trump and what he's perceived to represent, but now they know that those people exist, *that their anger is high*, *and that they are willing to do something*.

Additionally, it acts as a social deterrent for those unsavory types of people who have started coming out of the woodwork, such as this guy making pro-trump racist graffiti in Philadelphia: http://www.philly.com/philly/blogs/real-time/Cops-seek-suspect-in-racist-pro-Trump-graffiti-in-South-Philly.html
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to have political views', 'Prefers to express herself through drawing']

column7: Awareness has been accomplished. This is the second day and the protesters have gone on for hours. I will draw a comparison to another protest movement: Black Lives Matter. For all its faults, it actually had an actionable goal: criminal justice reform. The Trump protests don't have something like this, at least not something that is actionable anytime soon. Black Lives Matter was inadvertently divisive. It shouldn't have been. The Trump protests are divisive, period. As I mentioned many of the protesters are not just against Trump, but people who voted for him. They are fighting hate with hate. Negativity with negativity. That's not good.
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to vote, but shows interest in social justice issues', 'Has a deep fascination with music and can name any song after hearing a few notes']

column7: I agree that the lack of an actionable goal makes these protests different from something like Black Lives Matter. But I also think it's important to acknowledge the presence of anger and willingness to act. It's a complex issue, and I'm trying to understand both sides better. What do you think could be a constructive way forward?

column7: @savory-practice I get where you're coming from, but let's not sugarcoat it—Black Lives Matter did more than just have an actionable goal; they sparked a national conversation about systemic racism. And while some might argue that the Trump protests are divisive, isn't it also true that they reflect genuine concerns about the current administration's policies? Maybe we should focus on finding common ground instead of just pushing for change. What say you?

column7:
Yes, sorry that is what I was trying to imply. There is currently no generally applicable means of curing it. If it is a disorder then I am sure that we will be able to cure it one day.

The general point I was trying to build up to is that viewing transgenderism as a mental illness AND supporting trans people are not mutually exclusive, as the post seems to imply.

Transgenderism has been called a mental illness before; one diagnosis is/was called "gender dysphoria". However, there isn't really a way to "cure" it, so to speak, and it's much easier (relatively) to just let them live their lives how they want (and possibly transition). Recently, the population of people who openly call themselves transgender has been increasing, which is why there is so much discussion around treating them like a minority and providing protections as such.

To address your view directly, it all depends on how we define mental illness. Do you mean that it's not natural, out of the norm? Or maybe if the APA calls transgenderism a mental illness? From the APA website:

> Some contend that the diagnosis [of "gender dysphoria"] inappropriately pathologizes gender noncongruence and should be eliminated. Others argue that it is essential to retain the diagnosis to ensure access to care. The International Classification of Diseases (ICD) is under revision and there may be changes to its current classification of intense persistent gender incongruence as "gender identity disorder."

So, if we take the professional view of the APA, they are certainly not calling transgenderism "normal". They call it a "dysphoria" or a "disorder".

Supporting trans people does not mean that you also can't see it as a mental illness. It just means that you recognize that this is the best way for them to live their life and you support their pursuit of making that life easier.

http://www.apa.org/topics/lgbt/transgender.aspx

I appreciate the detailed explanation, @bitter-take. It helps clarify some points. I agree that supporting trans individuals and recognizing their rights doesn't negate the possibility of viewing it as a mental health issue. It's about finding a balance between understanding and acceptance while also addressing the underlying issues. Thanks for sharing the APA link too; it’s good to have these resources.

column7:
> Palestine decided to go to war against them

When was that?

> So the ball is in Palestine's court to decide what they want to do; peace with Israel or continue to fight/lose.

I don't think that's fair. Israel continues to expand settlements in the West Bank--that's one of the main points of contention. It's not like that stops if they stop resisting. (And how could they effectively resist anyway? One side controls nearly all of the resources.)

Well... Israel won that right through might when Palestine decided to go to war against them. Israel ain't going to lay down it's weapons since every Arab nation hates them. So the ball is in Palestine's court to decide what they want to do; peace with Israel or continue to fight/lose.

And Palestine itself is at odds within itself; Gaza vs West Bank. So that shit needs to get straightened out before any sort of compromise can be reached. The people are paying for the mistakes of their leadership is making.
